data.title: Trump defends data centers as he expands pledge to make them 'pay their own way'
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday expanded his pledge that aims to have data centers “pay their own way” for the electricity they use, but he took the opportunity to defend data centers at the same time.

Trump announced that entities ranging from utilities to state governments are joining the pledge, which was already signed by major tech companies.

The pledge, which is voluntary, says that data centers should either bring their own power or pay for the power and additional power resources and infrastructure that they use. It also says that they should invest in local communities and, when possible, allow their backup power to be available for communities in emergency situations.

While the pledge serves as an acknowledgement from the White House that data centers’ significant electricity use has the potential to jack up prices for consumers, Trump also praised the centers, saying that communities should get on board with them.

“You have communities that really want the data centers, and frankly, those are the smart communities because it means tremendous numbers of jobs, very little actual disruption,” he said.

He touted the “tremendous amounts of money” that tech companies are investing to build the infrastructure behind the AI boom.

“You have to convince your community how great these things [are],” Trump added. “You can’t fight it. You have to go with it, and you’re going to have all that protection.”

The White House also unveiled a website listing the pledge’s signatories. This included 27 data center developers, 55 utilities and 106 electric cooperatives. Twenty-three Republican governors have also signed the pledge, a White House official confirmed.

While the White House has touted the pledge as a way to reduce the centers’ impacts on consumers, both left-wing and right-wing critics expressed doubts that it will actually be effective in bringing down consumer costs.

“There’s no enforcement …. if you wanted to call somebody out for violating the pledge, what does that even mean?” asked Travis Fisher, director of energy and environmental policy studies at the libertarian Cato Institute.

The pledge’s website seeks to push back on this argument, acknowledging that skeptics have questioned whether it “could be enforced, given that power prices are set by state regulators, electricity buyers, and electricity sellers.”

“The Ratepayer Protection Pledge now has commitments from every one of them — and from the developers who actually build the facilities,” it reads.

Fisher also said that defining which costs belong to data center companies is complicated.

“Even if you agree in principle that you’re going to own all of your costs, that argument about which costs are actually yours versus someone else’s, or which things were built that could benefit you but also help others — that cost allocation fight is not going to get any simpler,” he said. 

He said that it makes sense to expand the pledge to states and utilities because “state commissions and utilities are the ones who set retail rates,” but governors’ power over state utility regulators may be limited. 

Meanwhile, left-wing critics have also slammed the pledge as “toothless.”

“I honestly think that this will result in higher prices because these companies will feel like they did something to get people off their back, and now they don’t have to worry about keeping rates under control,” said Climate Power senior advisor and former Biden White House official Jesse Lee. “All of it is not only toothless but completely vague, so there’s no specifics about what they’re actually supposed to do.”

data.title: DOJ will withdraw subpoenas against New York Times journalists over Air Force One reporting
data.description:
The Justice Department on Thursday said it would withdraw subpoenas issued to several New York Times reporters earlier this month over their reporting on security concerns regarding the new Air Force One jet gifted by Qatar.

The development came during a hearing in a Manhattan courtroom in which Judge Arun Subramanian was weighing whether to grant the outlet’s motion to quash, according to multiple reports.

“The government is prepared to unilaterally withdraw the subpoenas at this time,” Sean Buckley, the chief counsel to U.S. Attorney Jay Clayton, said at the hearing in U.S. District Court, according to the New York Times.

Four Times journalists —Julian E. Barnes, Eric Lipton, Tyler Pager and Eric Schmitt— were hit with subpoenas on July 10, seeking to compel their testimony before a federal grand jury.

The Justice Department also sought the reporters’ phone and text messaging records and, in some cases, those of family members, according to the newspaper.

The Trump administration was pushing to identify the confidential government sources who spoke with reporters about the donated jet’s security capabilities, a move critics see as a brazen attempt to chill press freedom and suppress unfavorable coverage. 

“The Subpoenas were issued in a bad faith effort to harass, intimidate, and retaliate against members of the press for reporting on subjects the President finds embarrassing,” lawyers for the newspaper wrote in a court filing.

Prosecutors in the Southern District of New York had initially asked Subramanian to pause enforcement of the subpoenas for two weeks while it pursues additional investigative steps.

Buckley contended in a Tuesday letter to the judge that the First Amendment does not shield reporters from appearing before a grand jury for questioning “as to their knowledge of criminal conduct, even as to the identities of the sources.”

Prosecutors also pushed back on the Times’s argument that proper legal procedures and Justice Department regulations were not followed in issuing the subpoenas, calling the allegations “inaccurate.”

“Without divulging classified or otherwise confidential and sensitive information, the movants are incorrect, for example, in asserting that the investigation lacks proper predication and that no investigative steps were taken prior to issuance of the subpoenas,” Buckley wrote on behalf of Clayton, adding that prosecutors considered “among other things, the underlying national security equities at stake.”

The newspaper reported earlier this month that President Trump flew on the old Air Force One jet when he left the NATO summit in Turkey because of a security precaution related to the war with Iran. 

The journalists also wrote later in the week that the new plane lacked the “defensive countermeasures” of older models, including advanced antimissile capabilities, citing multiple officials who had been briefed on the retrofitting.

The Justice Department previously told The Hill in a statement that the Times reporters were not its targets but “those leaking classified are.”

“We recognize there may always be natural tension there, but we are not going to ignore the law and stop investigating the people who work in the administration and think it’s OK to leak classified information impacting national security,” a spokesperson said.

data.title: Watch live: Trump welcomes Los Angeles Dodgers to White House
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday afternoon will host the Los Angeles Dodgers in the White House Rose Garden.

The 2025 World Series Champions clinched their 9th title last year against the Toronto Blue Jays, nabbing four out of the seven games.

The president congratulated the team after their victory last November, calling them “incredible CHAMPIONS!!!

“A lesser group of men would never have been able to win that game, or game 6, for that matter,” he wrote at the time on Truth Social. “So many stars made it all happen. Also, congratulations to ownership. What a great job they have done. SEE YOU ALL AT THE WHITE HOUSE!!!”

The event is scheduled to begin at 4:30 p.m. EDT.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Ocasio-Cortez: GOP's communist warnings 'very silly'
data.description:
Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.) on Thursday called Republicans’ warnings about a supposed communist threat are “very silly.”

Ocasio-Cortez laughed when asked if there were concerns over democratic socialism becoming “full-blown communism” during a gaggle with reporters on Capitol Hill.

“I think it’s very silly,” she added. “Just think about how impossible it has been to raise the minimum wage in America in 10, 15, 20 years. We can’t even raise the minimum wage –– do Republicans really think that this stuff is going to happen? No, they don’t.”

Ocasio-Cortez added that Republicans were “trying to scare people because they don’t want us talking about the fact that we all have the right to health care in this country.”

She continued that Republicans “want to call it communist and they want to call everything they don’t like ‘communist.”

“Because if people actually wake up to the fact that their elected officials are screwing them over when they get here, then they’re going to realize they deserve better and that health care, as a right, isn’t crazy,’ she added.

Republicans and some Democrats have fumed over the successes democratic socialist candidates have had in recent victories against incumbent Democrats during the 2026 midterm election season.

Democratic socialist candidates have seen victories in New York, Pennsylvania and Colorado, as well as major cities like Washington, D.C., and Los Angeles. These candidates have campaigned on addressing rising costs and inflation, holding the Trump administration accountable and against Israel’s partnership with the U.S. following the country’s war in Gaza.

President Trump conflating the candidates’ ideology with communism, said last month communism is the biggest threat facing the country, “maybe since our founding.”

House Republicans have repeated to the president’s claim, with some including Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) claiming that “these crazy little mini-Mamdani’s … are a danger to you and your family.”

“The radical left feels they need to hide what their policies are,” House Republican Conference Chair Lisa McClain (R-Mich.) said on Tuesday. “They soften the language. They invent nicer labels. They call themselves ‘democratic socialists,’ almost as though they’re ashamed to tell the American people what they actually believe.”

“Let’s call it what it is,” she added. “It’s communism.”

Secretary of State Marco Rubio last week, as he convened with officials from more than 60 countries, called for an “international response” to left-wing political violence. He said the U.S. will have its counterterrorism efforts shift to crack down on what he called “far-left terror.”

data.title: Live updates: House OKs, Senate rejects war powers resolution; DOJ will drop New York Times subpoenas
data.description:
President Trump is threatening to intensify the war with Iran as some Republican lawmakers urge him to “finish the job,” but there is also widespread skepticism on Capitol Hill on both sides of the aisle about whether he can deliver a victory.

The House narrowly approved a Democratic-sponsored war powers resolution with the help of four GOP lawmakers. Meanwhile, Republicans defeated a motion to advance a similar war powers resolution in the Senate. They come as Trump’s support for the Iran conflict is reaching a new low among his base.

The Iran-backed Houthi rebels in Yemen on Thursday attacked two oil tankers in the Red Sea, a ramping up of the conflict since the group announced a blockade of Saudi ships attempting to go through the Bab el-Mandeb Strait earlier this week. 

In a New York courtroom, federal prosecutors agreed to drop subpoenas issued to New York Times reporters over their coverage of the new Air Force One jet, the Associated Press reported.

Dr. Erica Schwartz faced a vote in the Senate Health Committee for her nomination to be director of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, but it was postponed due to attendance.

The Energy Department announced Wednesday a nuclear cooperation deal with Saudi Arabia, and it drew swift backlash. On Thursday morning, Trump added a condition: The Saudis must join the Abraham Accords. Insiders can read more here.

On Thursday afternoon, Trump will greet the 2025 World Series champion Los Angeles Dodgers at the White House.

Follow live below.

data.title: Blue states sue over new FEMA grant conditions
data.description:
Roughly two dozen Democratic-led states sued the Trump administration on Thursday over its latest set of conditions for receiving Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) grant funds. 

The states say the demands are without authority and relate to elections and immigration enforcement.

The conditions had made federal grants a key front in President Trump’s push to strengthen proof-of-citizenship requirements in voting, with the case adding to a monthslong legal clash between the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) and blue states over the strings attached to their federal dollars.

“Congress never gave DHS or FEMA authority to rewrite state election law, require cooperation with federal immigration agents, or terminate federal funding streams at any time and for any reason,” the states wrote in their lawsuit.

The Hill has reached out to a DHS spokesperson for comment.

Since Trump retook office, he has looked to prompt blue states to comply with his administration’s policy objectives by placing related conditions on longstanding grant programs.

To continue accessing one of FEMA’s largest funding pots, the lawsuit states the administration has demanded states verify the citizenship of their voter rolls, among other demands. The states called it a “faulty” system.

The lawsuit follows a judge’s ruling last year that FEMA unlawfully tied grants to immigration conditions and another ruling invalidating an effort to reallocate millions of dollars.

“Apparently undeterred by these judgments, DHS and FEMA have continued their campaign of coercion, imposing a raft of new conditions on the same grant programs,” the states wrote in their new lawsuit.

The new case is led by four Democratic state attorneys general: California’s Rob Bonta, Illinois’s Kwame Raoul, New Jersey’s Jennifer Davenport and Rhode Island’s Peter Neronha. They were joined by 19 others, the governors of Kentucky and Pennsylvania and the city of Washington, D.C.

“President Trump refuses to listen to the courts when they tell him that he cannot exceed the authority of his office, and he cannot extort states into doing what he wants,” Raoul said at a press conference.

data.title: US Navy restricting press access to sailors' service history
data.description:
The U.S. Navy will no longer release service history information for sailors, ending the decades-long practice to address what officials describe as security threats and harassment concerns.

The move comes amid greater press restrictions imposed by Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, which include a requirement for reporters to be escorted by authorized personnel in the Pentagon. 

Under the new policy, the Navy will typically withhold information such as a sailor’s duty assignments, awards and service history that was traditionally made available to journalists and the public upon request.

Capt. Candice Tresch, a spokesperson for the Chief of Naval Personnel, told The Associated Press that the Navy will continue to confirm whether an individual is a sailor only if the request is deemed of “high public interest.”

The policy follows guidance issued last week by Navy spokesperson Rear Adm. John Robinson, directing the service to remove public-facing information about commanding officers. Tresch told the AP that the policy was broadened to cover all sailors “in the interest of equality.”

When the AP had inquired about a biography of a Blue Angels pilot following a low-level flyover at a Florida beach last week, the outlet reported that the Navy cited the new policy when declining to provide the biography. 

The office refused despite information such as names, photographs and descriptions already appearing on the Blue Angels’ public website. Tresch later provided the biography to the AP after the outlet inquired further.

In a statement to the AP, Tresch said the Pentagon will now only release such details of the sailor if the person is “a public figure or extenuating circumstances exist.”

The new policy marks a broader push by the Pentagon to limit public access to military information. CBS News reported that the Pentagon asked Congress to create a new exemption under the Freedom of Information Act to be able to withhold unclassified access from public access.

data.title: Vance detail Secret Service agent suspected of leaking information put on administrative leave
data.description:
The U.S. Secret Service said Thursday it was investigating a member of Vice President Vance’s detail for allegedly leaking sensitive information.

“A member of the Vice Presidential Protective Division is the subject of an administrative investigation, and potential criminal inquiry, involving allegations of compromising operational and information security,” Secret Service Chief of Communications Anthony Guglielmi said in a statement to The Hill.

Guglielmi said the agency will not comment on any specifics about the case but added that “any conduct that potentially threatens the safety of a protectee will not be tolerated.”

The investigation comes one week after an MS NOW story reported on frustrations among Vance’s security detail over sudden and immediate trips and assignments. This included internal concerns “about Vance and his office pressing them for trips and assignments that some agents consider an inappropriate or even unprecedented use of government resources compared to prior vice presidents.”

The agency told the outlet that previous vice presidents “eschewed using such expensive government perks for the convenience of their children’s schedule, and agents typically drove children locally in sports utility vehicles.”

The story cited multiple sources, including unnamed Trump administration officials with knowledge of Vance’s flight plans, schedule and the agents’ “frustration,” particularly over last-minute travel requests from the vice president and second lady Usha Vance.

Current and former Secret Service personnel told MS NOW that such “off-the-record” trips require agents to cancel their own plans, including taking time off, and race to the location where they are needed while also quickly developing security plans.

“The detail is tired of them not giving notice on things and making everything an OTR,” one source familiar with the detail’s frustration told the outlet. “He [Vance] thinks he can still move around like a U.S. Senator.”

Frustration among the agents has resulted in some making custom coins and stickers mocking the vice president and his family’s irregular trips. MS NOW reviewed images with Vance’s Secret Service code name, “Bobcat,” used on coins reading “Bobcat OTR Survivors Club.”

CNN, which first reported the leaked investigation, reported that the agent placed on administrative leave is suspected of being the source for MS NOW’s story.

Similar leaks over the Qatari-gifted jet for Air Force One and security concerns reported in The New York Times prompted the Department of Justice (DOJ) to subpoena four of the outlet’s reporters.

The DOJ previously told The Hill that the reporters “are not the targets, those leaking classified information are.”

“We recognize there may always be natural tension there, but we are not going to ignore the law and stop investigating the people who work in the administration and think it’s OK to leak classified information impacting national security,” the spokesperson said.

data.title: Warren presses Trump CFPB nominee on conflicts of interest, workforce cuts
data.description:
Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-Mass.) on Thursday pressed President Trump’s nominee to lead the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) regarding conflicts of interest and the Trump administration’s cuts to the agency. 

During Brian Johnson’s nomination hearing in front of the Senate Banking Committee, ranking member Warren noted the consumer protection watchdog, under acting director Russell Vought, dropped a lawsuit against Capital One last year after the company donated $1 million to Trump’s inaugural committee.

The initial CFPB lawsuit, which the agency filed during the final days of the Biden administration, alleged Capital One cheated consumers out of more than $2 billion in interest payments on their savings accounts. 

Warren asked Johnson, “Will you commit to notifying Congress and the inspector general [of the Federal Reserve] immediately if the White House contacts you about an enforcement action against a Trump family company or a big donor that cheated families?”

Johnson replied that during his two-plus years at the CFPB during the first Trump administration, such a scenario “never happened.” 

The Massachusetts Democrat followed up, “That’s not my question. My question is, if it does, will you notify Congress, and will you notify the inspector general?”

She continued, “Look, there’s obviously a problem here. I’m looking for a way to clean it up,” and repeated her question to Johnson. But the nominee disputed the premise of Warren’s question.

“I don’t care whether you like the premise. I have given you a set of facts that are not made up. It’s happening right now,” Warren remarked, pointing at the dais for emphasis.

“I want your commitment that if it happens on your watch, you’ll pick up the phone and call us,” the progressive lawmaker added, gesturing to her fellow senators on the panel. “Our job is oversight. That’s not so hard. Are you willing to do that?”

Johnson then said he was “not going to engage in a hypothetical,” but he could not finish his sentence before Warren cut him off. 

“Ok, so the answer is no. You will not try to hose out the corruption,” Warren said. 

Since November 2024, Johnson has worked as Capital One’s vice president of U.S. card compliance, according to his LinkedIn. While Trump nominated him to serve as director of the CFPB last month, Johnson acknowledged to Warren he still works at the bank. 

When the Massachusetts Democrat said Capital One is paying Johnson as he is testifying, the nominee said he was appearing before the panel in his “personal” capacity. 

“Okay,” Warren replied, chuckling. “But they’re paying you to get confirmed for this job. I understand they’re also letting you keep a bonus that you have earned when you leave.

“You know, they must be really excited about getting you confirmed here.”

Johnson signed an agreement earlier this month pledging to recuse himself from any matters involving Capital One for two years if he is confirmed, Banking Dive reported on Tuesday.

Multiple Democratic senators, including Warren, also questioned Johnson about the cuts Vought has proposed to the CFPB’s workforce during his 17-plus months in charge.

A federal appeals court last month blocked the Trump administration from immediately firing bureau employees, but a federal judge on July 10 paused the union lawsuit seeking to block the administration from shutting down the watchdog.

The ruling from U.S. District Judge Amy Bernan Jackson, an appointee of former President Obama, allows for Johnson to decide how to move forward regarding cuts if the Senate confirms him. 

When Democratic Sen. Chris Van Hollen (Md.) asked Johnson whether he, like Vought, believes the administration should “eliminate” the CFPB, the nominee said that is not his “intention.”

“The CFPB is a creature of statute. Congress has assigned to it important law to implement and execute, and my intention is to execute the law,” Johnson said.

But when Sen. Ruben Gallego (D-Ariz.) asked Johnson near the end of the hearing whether Vought has done anything he disagrees with, the nominee said, “None come to mind at the moment.”

data.title: O'Reilly: Trump should 'take the next month off'
data.description:
Political commentator Bill O’Reilly on Wednesday said President Trump should “take the next month off” as the 80-year-old chief executive faces widening foreign and domestic dilemmas.

O’Reilly, 76, wrapped up the latest episode of his podcast, “No Spin News,” with a reflection on the roughly 40 years that he has known the president. He said he has noticed Trump “hunching over a little bit and that’s normal,” before prescribing his recommendation to the president.

“So my advice to Donald Trump is to take the next month off if you can get into an environment where you like golf and relax a little bit,” O’Reilly said. “You got to deal with Iran, I understand that. Everybody understands it. But you can do that on the phone and you can do that with your people coming.”

The former Fox News host added that Trump needs “a breather.”

“I need a breather and I’m going to build it into August. But he needs a breather much more than I do,” O’Reilly said, adding that he wishes the best for Trump and all of his predecessors.

The remarks come as Trump sees conflict in the Middle East widening with the Houthis striking two Saudi Arabian oil tankers in the Red Sea, an alternate passage for oil exports after Iran shut down the Strait of Hormuz in response to the resumption of hostilities between the Islamic regime and the U.S.

These closures have impacted the path of transit for oil exports, causing energy prices to soar with the U.S. national gas average reaching $4.09 on Thursday, according to AAA.

Trump’s health has also been in the spotlight since before his return to the White House last year. Before turning 80 last month, bruising on his hands and swelling of his ankles raised questions about his physical fitness. Questions were also raised by critics following several instances of the president closing his eyes during events in the Oval Office.

White House physician Sean Barbabella said in July 2024 that the president was diagnosed with chronic venous insufficiency, noting that the bruising was “consistent with minor soft tissue damage from frequent handshaking.”

Trump himself also criticized and mocked former President Biden’s health, fixating on his use of an autopen to raise questions about Biden’s cognitive abilities while in office. Biden, at the time, was the oldest man inaugurated at 78 years and 61 days old before Trump returned to the White House at 78 and 220 days old.
